title: Analyzing a Tool Failure
concept: Tracing how an argument type error caused a tool crash, and how it was successfully resolved.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: what is a "tool" when an AI agent uses one?

options.text: An outside function the AI can call to do a task, like search or math
A hidden password inside the AI's brain
A drawing the AI makes for fun

exercises.explain: A tool is an outside function the agent can call, such as a calculator, a web search, or a weather lookup. The AI decides which tool to use and what to send it.

cards.title: The Crash Report
cards.body: An AI agent tried to book a flight, but the tool crashed instantly. No booking, no answer, just an error. What went wrong?

cards.title: Argument Type Error
cards.body: A tool expects each input in a certain form, like a number or text. An argument type error happens when the AI sends the wrong form, such as text where a number belongs.

cards.title: Wrong-Shaped Plug
cards.body: It's like trying to push a round plug into a square socket. The wall outlet expects one shape; give it the wrong shape and nothing works.

cards.title: Trace the Failure
cards.body: The tool wanted seats as the number 2. The agent sent the word "two" instead. The tool couldn't do math on a word, so it crashed. The fix: send 2 as a real number.

cards.title: Not Always the Tool's Fault
cards.body: People assume a crash means the tool is broken. Often the tool is fine, the AI just handed it the wrong type of input.

cards.title: Before vs After the Fix
cards.body: The tool only accepts a real number for seats. Watch what the agent sends.

exercises.prompt: A weather tool needs a city name as text, but the agent sent the number 90210. What likely happens?

options.text: An argument type error, because it got a number where text was expected

exercises.explain: The tool expected a city name written as text. A number is the wrong type, so the tool can't process it and throws an argument type error.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Sending the word "three" to a tool that expects the number 3 can cause a crash.
exercises.explain: The tool wants a real number to do math with. The word "three" is text, so the tool can't use it and may crash. Sending 3 as a number fixes it.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about how the failure was fixed.
exercises.sentence: The agent resent the seat count as a real number instead of text, and the crash was resolved.
exercises.explain: Once the agent sent the correct type (a number), the tool could run, so the failure was resolved.

exercises.prompt: Which crash explanation was written by an AI to sound like a clear debug note?

options.text: Tool call failed: 'seats' expected a number but received text "two"; converting the value to 2 resolved the error.
idk it just broke lol try again maybe
The computer got tired so it stopped working today.

exercises.explain: The AI-written note clearly names the argument, the expected type, the wrong value, and the fix. The others are vague or make-believe.

exercises.prompt: An agent calls a payment tool. It sends amount as "50 dollars" (text) but the tool needs a plain number like 50. The tool crashes. Which sequence correctly traces and resolves this?

options.text: Wrong type sent (text, not number) → tool can't process it → crash → resend amount as 50 → works
Tool is broken forever → delete the agent → give up
The crash means the amount was too large → lower it to 1
Tools accept anything → nothing needed fixing

exercises.explain: To analyze a tool failure, trace it step by step: the agent sent the wrong type (text like "50 dollars" instead of the number 50), so the tool couldn't process the input and crashed. The fix is to send the value in the type the tool expects. Most tool crashes are input-type mismatches, not broken tools, and they're resolved by giving the tool exactly the shape it asks for.
